content.text: Earning a certification — SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, ISO 42001, or a combination — is not a one-time event. Every standard imposes a structured maintenance cadence: evidence collection windows, surveillance audits, management reviews, and periodic recertification cycles. For teams building and operating agentic AI systems, understanding the full maintenance rhythm from day zero through the three-year recertification cycle determines how compliance work is distributed across the engineering calendar and what evidence collection must be automated versus done manually.
What It Is

Certification maintenance is the set of ongoing activities required to keep a certification valid after initial issuance. It consists of three interrelated components: continuous evidence collection (ensuring audit evidence exists for every active control period), surveillance audits (periodic third-party reviews that verify controls remain effective between initial certification and full recertification), and the recertification cycle (the full audit that renews the certificate, typically every three years for ISO standards).

Each certification type has its own cadence rules. SOC 2 Type II operates on an annual observation window model — the auditor reviews controls over a defined time period (typically 6–12 months), so evidence must exist continuously throughout that window. ISO 27001 and ISO 42001 use a three-year certification cycle with annual surveillance audits that sample approximately one-third of controls per visit. Combining both certifications (as most AI product companies do) creates a consolidated calendar with distinct milestones for each standard.
Why It Matters

Certification lapses — whether from missed surveillance audit scheduling, expired certificates, or failed audits due to evidence gaps — directly impact commercial relationships. Enterprise buyers often include active certification status as a contractual prerequisite. A lapsed SOC 2 report can trigger contract reviews, delay renewals, and require emergency re-engagement of auditors at premium rates. For regulated industry customers (financial services, healthcare), a compliance gap can trigger their own regulatory exposure if the vendor's controls fail.

Beyond commercial risk, the maintenance cadence serves a genuine governance function. Security and AI governance risks evolve continuously: new attack vectors emerge, models drift, data pipelines change, and regulatory requirements are updated. The annual surveillance audit creates a forcing function that drives regular internal review — identifying control gaps before they become audit findings and ensuring that the AIMS or ISMS documentation accurately reflects the current system rather than its state at initial certification.

For engineering teams, the maintenance cadence is an opportunity to systematize evidence collection. Teams that treat compliance evidence as an engineering deliverable — generating it continuously from CI/CD pipelines, cloud audit logs, and automated testing — experience far less audit-period stress than teams that rely on manual evidence collection sprints in the weeks before each audit.
Benefits

Continuous commercial readiness: Active certifications allow the sales team to respond to security questionnaires with a certificate link rather than assembling ad-hoc documentation for each prospect.

Structured governance cadence: Surveillance audits and management reviews impose a regular governance rhythm that keeps security and AI risk management active rather than dormant between certification events.
Incremental audit workload: Spreading evidence collection and control reviews across the year distributes the compliance workload rather than creating an acute peak before each audit.

Early detection of control gaps: Regular internal audits (required by both ISO 27001 Clause 9.2 and ISO 42001 Clause 9.2) surface control failures before they become major nonconformities in the external audit.

Evidence of maturity to regulators: A maintained three-year audit history demonstrates organizational commitment to governance rather than a point-in-time assessment, which is increasingly valued by regulators reviewing AI governance programs.

Reduced recertification effort: Organizations that maintain continuous evidence and perform strong surveillance cycles experience faster and less disruptive recertification audits because the evidence library is already current.
Trade-offs

Ongoing cost: Annual surveillance audits and SOC 2 renewals carry auditor fees that recur indefinitely. For early-stage companies, cumulative annual compliance costs (SOC 2 + ISO 27001 + ISO 42001) can reach six figures per year when including auditor fees, GRC platform subscriptions, and internal staff time.

Calendar dependency: Certification Body (CB) availability can create scheduling constraints, particularly for surveillance audits. CBs with heavy portfolios may offer limited audit windows, requiring organizations to plan 3–6 months ahead.

Evidence continuity risk: A control that is only partially implemented for even one month within a SOC 2 observation window can create an exception finding. Unlike ISO surveillance audits that sample point-in-time, SOC 2 Type II tests control effectiveness over the entire observation period.

Documentation staleness: As systems evolve, documentation (AI system impact assessments, data flow diagrams, tool registries) can fall behind actual system state. Surveillance auditors who find material discrepancies between documentation and observed reality will raise nonconformities.

Scope creep risk: Adding new AI systems or infrastructure without formal scope extension processes creates gaps where uncertified systems operate alongside certified ones — confusing both internal teams and customers about what is actually covered.
How It Works
SOC 2 Type II Annual Cycle

SOC 2 Type II does not have a certification expiry in the ISO sense — instead it has an observation period. The auditor tests whether controls operated effectively throughout a specified window, typically twelve months. At the end of the window, the CPA firm issues a report. To maintain continuous SOC 2 coverage, the next observation period begins immediately after the previous one ends. Most organizations align their SOC 2 observation period with their fiscal year.

Evidence must exist for every control throughout the observation period. If a control (for example, multi-factor authentication enforcement) was disabled for two weeks due to a system migration, that period becomes an exception in the report. Controls that are detective (log review, security alert review) require evidence of regular execution — typically weekly or monthly records of who performed the review and what was found.
ISO 27001 and ISO 42001 Three-Year Cycle

ISO certification follows a three-year cycle: initial certification audit (Stage 1 + Stage 2), followed by two annual surveillance audits (Year 1 and Year 2), followed by recertification (Year 3). Each surveillance audit samples approximately one-third of the applicable Annex A controls, though the CB has discretion to focus on previously identified weaknesses or areas of known change. By the end of the three-year cycle, all controls will have been audited at least once.

The recertification audit at Year 3 is similar in scope to the initial certification: the CB reviews whether the management system is still appropriate, adequate, and effective, and conducts a Stage 2-style effectiveness assessment. Organizations with strong evidence libraries and clean surveillance histories typically complete recertification in one day per system in scope.

attrs.alt: Certification lifecycle timeline showing SOC 2 Type II annual cycle and ISO 27001/42001 three-year cycle with surveillance audits and recertification milestone

content.text: Management Review (ISO Clause 9.3)

Both ISO 27001 and ISO 42001 require documented management reviews at planned intervals (at minimum annually). The management review must address: results of internal audits, nonconformities and corrective actions, monitoring and measurement results, results of risk assessments, opportunities for continual improvement, and relevant changes to the external environment (regulatory updates, threat landscape changes, business context). The review output — decisions on improvement actions, resource needs, and policy updates — must be documented and retained as audit evidence.
Architecture of a Maintenance Program

A well-designed maintenance program structures evidence collection, review activities, and audit scheduling into four time horizons: continuous (automated), monthly (operational), quarterly (governance), and annual (audit).
Continuous Evidence Collection

The most resilient maintenance programs automate evidence collection at the source. Cloud provider audit logs (AWS CloudTrail, GCP Audit Logs) capture access and configuration events continuously. CI/CD pipelines preserve build artifacts, test results, and deployment approval records. Monitoring systems maintain metric history. GRC platforms (Vanta, Drata) pull this evidence via API integration, map it to controls, and flag gaps in real time. For agentic AI systems, agent decision logs and tool invocation records are additional evidence streams that must be captured and retained.
Monthly Operational Reviews

Monthly operational reviews address detective controls: reviewing security alerts, checking access control lists for terminated employees, reviewing change management logs, and validating that AI system monitoring dashboards are within threshold. These reviews must be documented — a log entry in the GRC platform or a signed-off ticket in the issue tracker — because the auditor will sample these records across the observation period.
Quarterly Governance Reviews

Quarterly reviews address governance-level controls: risk register updates, vendor security reviews, AI system impact assessment updates for systems that have undergone material changes, and policy reviews. Quarterly is the appropriate cadence for AI governance controls because agentic systems typically change faster than annual review cycles can track.
When to Use This Approach

The continuous maintenance model is appropriate for any organization committed to maintaining active certifications. The specific tooling choices — GRC platform vs. manual tracking, automated evidence collection vs. manual — should be calibrated to the organization's size and audit complexity. A 20-person company maintaining SOC 2 + ISO 27001 might use a lightweight GRC platform with manual quarterly reviews. A 500-person company maintaining SOC 2 + ISO 27001 + ISO 42001 across ten AI systems needs automated evidence collection from CI/CD pipelines and a GRC platform with policy workflow management.

For teams considering whether to maintain a combined SOC 2 + ISO 27001 + ISO 42001 program versus a subset: the overhead of combined programs is lower than running them independently because internal audits, management reviews, and policy documentation can be shared. The incremental cost of adding ISO 42001 to an existing ISO 27001 program is typically 20–40% of the standalone ISO 42001 cost.
How to Implement
Setting up a maintenance program requires both process design and tooling configuration:

Build the compliance calendar: Map every required audit activity — SOC 2 observation period start/end, ISO surveillance audit windows, management review dates, internal audit dates — onto the engineering calendar for the next 36 months. Share this with engineering leadership so compliance events are not surprised by product release dates.

Automate continuous evidence collection: Configure GRC platform integrations (or custom scripts) to pull cloud logs, CI/CD artifacts, and monitoring data into the evidence library automatically. Validate monthly that evidence is being collected for each control.

Define evidence retention policy: SOC 2 auditors typically request evidence from throughout the observation period; ISO auditors may request evidence from any point since the last audit. Retain evidence for the full three-year certification cycle plus one additional year as a buffer.

Establish a change notification process: When a material change occurs (model version upgrade, new tool added to the agent, data pipeline architecture change), trigger an AIIA review and update the relevant control documentation before the change is deployed to production.

Schedule internal audits before external surveillance: Conduct an internal audit 4–8 weeks before each external surveillance audit. This gives time to identify and close control gaps before the auditor arrives, converting potential major nonconformities into closed corrective actions.

Track nonconformity closure: Every nonconformity — from internal audits, surveillance audits, or management reviews — must have a documented corrective action with an owner, due date, and closure evidence. Open nonconformities at audit time are automatic findings.
Examples
Compliance Calendar Structure

# compliance-calendar.yaml
# 36-month certification maintenance calendar
# Organization: Acme AI (SOC 2 + ISO 27001 + ISO 42001)

certification_periods:
  soc2_type_ii:
    observation_start: "2024-01-01"
    observation_end: "2024-12-31"
    report_expected: "2025-02-28"   # ~2 months for auditor to issue report
    next_observation_start: "2025-01-01"
    auditor: "CPA Firm: Grant Thornton"

  iso_27001:
    initial_cert_date: "2023-03-15"
    surveillance_1:
      scheduled: "2024-03-01"
      window: "2024-03-01 to 2024-03-03"
      cb: "BSI Group"
    surveillance_2:
      scheduled: "2025-03-01"
      window: "2025-03-01 to 2025-03-03"
    recertification:
      scheduled: "2026-03-01"
      window: "2026-03-01 to 2026-03-05"  # full stage 1+2

  iso_42001:
    initial_cert_date: "2024-01-10"
    surveillance_1:
      scheduled: "2025-01-08"
      window: "2025-01-08 to 2025-01-09"
      controls_sampled: "A.2, A.6, A.7.2, A.9"   # CB to confirm
    surveillance_2:
      scheduled: "2026-01-07"
      controls_sampled: "A.5, A.7.3, A.8, A.10"
    recertification:
      scheduled: "2027-01-06"

recurring_activities:
  monthly:
    - name: "Security alert review"
      owner: "security-lead"
      evidence: "Monthly Alert Review ticket in Jira"
    - name: "Access control review (IAM)"
      owner: "sre-lead"
      evidence: "IAM review artifact in Vanta"
    - name: "Agent decision log review"
      owner: "ai-governance-lead"
      evidence: "AI Ops dashboard screenshot + sign-off"

  quarterly:
    - name: "Risk register review"
      owner: "ciso + ai-governance-lead"
      evidence: "Updated risk register + meeting minutes"
    - name: "AI system impact assessment review"
      owner: "ai-governance-lead"
      evidence: "AIIA diff in Git + review approval"
    - name: "Vendor security review"
      owner: "security-lead"
      evidence: "Vendor questionnaire responses in GRC"

  annually:
    - name: "Management review"
      owner: "ciso + cto"
      evidence: "Management review minutes + action items"
    - name: "Internal audit (full)"
      owner: "internal-auditor"
      evidence: "Internal audit report"
    - name: "Policy review (all policies)"
      owner: "compliance-lead"
      evidence: "Policy versions with approval signatures"
    - name: "AI use policy refresh"
      owner: "legal + ai-governance-lead"
      evidence: "Policy changelog + board approval record"
Automated Evidence Freshness Check

# scripts/check_evidence_freshness.py
# Flags controls where evidence has not been collected within the required cadence.
# Run weekly in CI; alert on Slack if any control is stale.

import json
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from pathlib import Path

EVIDENCE_MANIFEST = Path("compliance/evidence-manifest.json")

CADENCE_DAYS = {
    "soc2-cc6.1-mfa-enforcement": 1,         # continuous — should have daily log entry
    "soc2-cc7.2-security-alert-review": 31,  # monthly
    "iso27001-a9.2-access-review": 31,        # monthly
    "iso42001-a7.2-hitl-checkpoint-log": 7,  # weekly for agentic system
    "iso42001-a9.5-model-monitoring": 1,      # continuous
    "iso42001-a6.1-aiia-review": 90,          # quarterly
    "iso27001-a5.1-policy-review": 365,       # annual
}

def check_freshness(manifest_path: Path) -> list[dict]:
    with open(manifest_path) as f:
        manifest = json.load(f)

    today = datetime.today()
    stale = []

    for control_id, cadence_days in CADENCE_DAYS.items():
        last_evidence = manifest.get(control_id, {}).get("last_collected_at")
        if not last_evidence:
            stale.append({
                "control": control_id,
                "status": "MISSING",
                "message": "No evidence collected yet",
                "cadence_days": cadence_days,
            })
            continue

        last_dt = datetime.fromisoformat(last_evidence)
        age_days = (today - last_dt).days
        threshold = int(cadence_days * 1.1)  # 10% grace period

        if age_days > threshold:
            stale.append({
                "control": control_id,
                "status": "STALE",
                "last_collected": last_evidence,
                "age_days": age_days,
                "max_age_days": cadence_days,
                "message": f"Evidence is {age_days - cadence_days} days overdue",
            })

    return stale

if __name__ == "__main__":
    stale = check_freshness(EVIDENCE_MANIFEST)
    if stale:
        print(f"ALERT: {len(stale)} control(s) have stale or missing evidence:")
        for item in stale:
            print(f"  {item['control']}: {item['status']} — {item['message']}")
        exit(1)
    else:
        print("All controls have current evidence within cadence requirements.")
        exit(0)
Pre-Audit Nonconformity Tracker

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/preaudit-readiness.sh
# Run 6 weeks before each surveillance or recertification audit.
# Outputs a readiness checklist and flags open nonconformities.

echo "=== Pre-Audit Readiness Check ==="
echo "Date: $(date)"
echo ""

# 1. Check for open nonconformities in the issue tracker
OPEN_NCRS=$(gh issue list --label "nonconformity" --state open --json number,title | jq length)
echo "Open Nonconformities: $OPEN_NCRS"
if [ "$OPEN_NCRS" -gt "0" ]; then
    echo "WARNING: Open NCRs must be closed or have documented corrective action plans before audit"
    gh issue list --label "nonconformity" --state open --json number,title | jq '.[] | "  #\(.number): \(.title)"' -r
fi

echo ""

# 2. Check management review was conducted within last 12 months
LAST_MGMT_REVIEW=$(cat compliance/management-review-dates.txt | tail -1)
echo "Last Management Review: $LAST_MGMT_REVIEW"
DAYS_SINCE=$(( ($(date +%s) - $(date -d "$LAST_MGMT_REVIEW" +%s)) / 86400 ))
if [ "$DAYS_SINCE" -gt "365" ]; then
    echo "WARNING: Management review is overdue ($DAYS_SINCE days since last review)"
fi

echo ""

# 3. Check internal audit was conducted since last external audit
LAST_INTERNAL_AUDIT=$(cat compliance/internal-audit-dates.txt | tail -1)
echo "Last Internal Audit: $LAST_INTERNAL_AUDIT"

# 4. Check policy freshness
find docs/policies/ -name "*.md" -newer compliance/.last-policy-review | while read f; do
    echo "Policy updated but not marked reviewed: $f"
done

echo ""
echo "=== Readiness check complete. Review warnings before scheduling external audit. ==="
Real-World Example

A Series B AI security company maintained SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, and ISO 42001 simultaneously for its agentic threat detection platform. The company deployed a GRC platform (Vanta) with automated integrations to AWS CloudTrail, GitHub Actions, Okta, and their Datadog monitoring stack. Continuous evidence collection covered approximately 60% of controls automatically. The remaining 40% — management reviews, AI system impact assessments, tool authorization matrix reviews, and vendor security questionnaires — required manual execution on a documented cadence.

Their annual surveillance audit cadence was structured so that the ISO 27001 and ISO 42001 surveillances occurred in the same two-week window in March each year, with the same Certification Body (BSI) reviewing both standards. This integrated audit approach saved approximately 30% of the combined audit cost compared to running them separately. Their SOC 2 observation period ran January through December, with the CPA firm report issued in February. The net result was two weeks of intensive audit activity per year (March) and the SOC 2 report preparation cycle from November through February — a manageable rhythm with no unexpected compliance peaks.

When the company's agentic threat detection system underwent a major architecture change — replacing a deterministic rule engine with an LLM-based decision-making layer — the compliance team triggered a formal scope extension process. This included updating the AI system impact assessment, adding new controls for LLM-specific risks (prompt injection, hallucination in alert triage), and notifying the Certification Body of the material change before the next surveillance window. The proactive notification prevented the architecture change from being treated as an undisclosed material change — a finding that could have jeopardized the existing certificates.
Common Mistakes

Treating the initial certification as the end of compliance work: Teams that achieve certification and then deprioritize compliance until the next audit create evidence gaps that become major nonconformities in the first surveillance audit. Compliance must be maintained continuously from day zero post-certification.

Missing the SOC 2 observation period start date: SOC 2 Type II reports only cover the observation period. If the auditor is engaged in month three of a twelve-month period, the first two months of evidence are lost. Always start the next observation period immediately when the previous one ends.

Failing to update the AIIA when the AI system changes: Model upgrades, new tool additions, and data pipeline changes are material changes to the AI system. Each requires an updated AI system impact assessment before the change is deployed. Waiting until the annual review creates a gap where the AIIA documents a system that no longer exists.

Not retaining evidence long enough: ISO surveillance auditors may ask for evidence from any point since the last audit — not just from the most recent period. Retain all control evidence for the full three-year certification cycle plus a buffer year. Purging evidence annually for storage cost reasons creates gaps that auditors find.

Scheduling the surveillance audit too close to the certificate expiry: ISO certificates expire three years from the initial certification date. If the recertification audit is not completed before expiry, the certificate lapses. Schedule recertification audits 2–3 months before the expiry date to allow time for nonconformity correction if needed.

Not coordinating ISO 27001 and ISO 42001 audits: Running ISO 27001 and ISO 42001 surveillance audits in separate windows with separate audit teams doubles the audit disruption and cost. Coordinate with the Certification Body to run them in the same window with an integrated audit team.
Best Practices

Set calendar reminders 90, 60, and 30 days before each audit event: Evidence that is missing or incomplete 90 days before an audit can be collected; evidence that is missing 7 days before cannot. Build the compliance calendar at the start of each year and distribute it to all control owners.

Conduct a mock audit 4–6 weeks before each external audit: Have the internal auditor (or a compliance-experienced team member) run through the control evidence library as if they were the external auditor. Document gaps and assign owners. Closing gaps takes time; a 4-week runway is the minimum practical resolution window.

Version-control all policy documents: Store policies in Git with tagged releases. This provides an immutable audit trail showing when policies were approved, who approved them, and what changed between versions — precisely what auditors request when verifying policy review cadence.

Define explicit control ownership: Every control in the control library must have a named owner responsible for evidence collection and control execution. Controls without owners accumulate evidence debt. Review ownership assignments when team members change roles.

Formalize the material change process: Define a lightweight process for identifying and processing material changes — architecture changes, new AI systems, scope extensions, significant vendor changes. The process should trigger AIIA updates, policy reviews, and Certification Body notification where required. Aim for a process that takes no more than two business days for typical changes so it does not become an obstacle to product delivery.

Use surveillance audits to expand scope efficiently: Each surveillance audit is an opportunity to add new AI systems to the certification scope without a full recertification. Work with the CB to include a targeted scope extension in the surveillance audit agenda. This keeps the scope current without requiring special-purpose audits.
Performance and Scalability

The compliance maintenance program scales on two dimensions: the number of certifications maintained and the number of in-scope systems. As both grow, the GRC platform becomes the critical scaling tool. Platforms like Vanta, Drata, and Thoropass support multi-framework management — mapping a single control to evidence requirements across SOC 2, ISO 27001, and ISO 42001 simultaneously — and allow per-system scoping so that new AI systems can be added to the evidence library without reconfiguring the entire program.

For engineering teams, the key scalability investment is in automated evidence collection. Automating 60–70% of control evidence collection reduces the compliance marginal cost of adding new AI systems to near-zero: cloud logs, CI/CD artifacts, and monitoring data scale with the system count automatically. The non-automatable controls (management reviews, AIIA reviews, vendor assessments) do scale with system count, but at a predictable and plannable rate.

Surveillance audit cost scales with scope. CB fees are typically time-based (per auditor-day). Adding ten AI systems to scope without organizational simplification — standardized architectures, shared tool registries, common monitoring dashboards — can increase surveillance audit time proportionally. Organizations that standardize their AI deployment architecture (common observability stack, standardized tool permission framework) make each in-scope AI system faster to audit, keeping audit day counts manageable as the portfolio grows.

Comparison with Alternatives

An alternative to full continuous certification maintenance is a point-in-time compliance attestation — a self-assessment questionnaire (SAQ) or a SOC 2 Type I report — which documents the design of controls at a single point in time rather than their effectiveness over a period. Point-in-time attestations are faster and cheaper to produce but provide weaker assurance: they cannot demonstrate that controls operated consistently throughout the period, which is what enterprise buyers and regulators require.

Another alternative is a shared assessment model (e.g., SIG Questionnaire, Consensus Assessments Initiative Questionnaire) where the organization responds to a standardized security questionnaire annually. These are less rigorous than third-party audits but are accepted by some buyers. The maintenance overhead is lower but the commercial positioning is weaker — a SOC 2 Type II report or ISO certificate carries more weight than a self-assessed questionnaire.

The integrated multi-framework approach (SOC 2 + ISO 27001 + ISO 42001 maintained simultaneously) carries the highest maintenance burden but the strongest combined assurance signal. For AI product companies targeting enterprise and regulated-industry customers, this combination is increasingly the expected standard rather than a competitive differentiator.
Security and Reliability

The compliance maintenance program is itself a security control. Regular management reviews and internal audits are detective controls that surface security and AI governance failures before they are identified by external attackers or regulators. The evidence retention requirements also serve a forensic function: in the event of a security incident or AI-related harm event, the retained audit log — agent decision logs, tool invocation records, access control change history — provides the evidentiary record for investigation and regulatory response.

For agentic AI systems specifically, the continuous evidence collection requirement for AI-specific controls (agent decision logs, model monitoring metrics, HITL checkpoint records) doubles as operational monitoring infrastructure. Teams that build this infrastructure for compliance purposes gain operational visibility that improves incident detection and mean-time-to-resolution independently of the compliance value.
Mental Model

Think of certification maintenance like maintaining a car's roadworthiness certification. The annual inspection (MOT in the UK, inspection sticker in the US) does not guarantee the car is safe all year — it verifies it was safe at the inspection date. But unlike a car which can degrade silently, a well-maintained compliance program instruments the vehicle continuously: checking oil pressure, tire pressure, and engine temperature in real time so that problems are detected before the next inspection rather than revealed at it. The surveillance audit is then a confirmation of what continuous monitoring already knows, not a discovery exercise.
Key Takeaways

SOC 2 Type II operates on an annual observation window — evidence must exist continuously throughout the period, not just be producible at audit time. Missing evidence for even a few weeks becomes an audit exception.

ISO 27001 and ISO 42001 use a three-year certification cycle: initial certification, two annual surveillance audits sampling approximately one-third of controls each, then recertification. Coordinate both standards with the same Certification Body in the same audit window to reduce cost and disruption.

Automate 60–70% of control evidence collection from cloud logs, CI/CD pipelines, and monitoring systems; reserve manual effort for governance-level controls (management reviews, AI system impact assessment reviews, vendor assessments) that require human judgment.

Material AI system changes — model version upgrades, new tools added to agent scope, data pipeline architecture changes — must trigger AIIA updates and CB notification before deployment, not at the next scheduled review.
Conduct an internal mock audit 4–6 weeks before every external surveillance audit. Gaps identified with a four-week runway can be closed; gaps identified one week before the audit cannot.

Build the 36-month compliance calendar at the start of each year, share it with engineering leadership, and align the SOC 2 observation period end and ISO surveillance audit windows to avoid compliance peaks coinciding with major product launches.
Retain all audit evidence for the full three-year certification cycle plus a buffer year; audit evidence is also forensic evidence for incident response and regulatory inquiries.
